title: Deep Copy Check
description: JSON clone limits vs structuredClone for snapshots.

questions.explanation: JSON serialization turns `Date` into an ISO string. JSON cloning is lossy for many types.
`JSON.stringify` throws on `BigInt`. That is another reason JSON cloning is not a universal deep-copy solution.

questions.prompt: What happens to `undefined` values in nested objects when using `JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(x))`?
questions.explanation: JSON cannot represent `undefined`. During serialization, object properties with `undefined` values are omitted.

questions.prompt: After `const y = structuredClone(x)` where `x = { when: new Date(), set: new Set([1, 2]) }`, which is true?
questions.explanation: `structuredClone` preserves supported built-in types like `Date` and `Set`, unlike JSON cloning.

questions.prompt: When should you prefer a deep copy over shallow copy + copy-on-write?

options.text: When you need an immutable snapshot boundary and cannot risk shared nested references
For every variable assignment in the program
Never — deep copy is always wrong

questions.explanation: Deep copies are expensive. Use them when you truly need isolation; otherwise prefer targeted copy-on-write updates.
